This guy claims that if you lose the remote control, you can still turn off the TV using just a piece of paper and a lighter. But is that true or just fiction? Let's test it. The remote control works by sending an infrared signal to a sensor in the TV, and this guy used a lighter to create an infrared light source. He made some cuts in the paper and waved it in front of the light, imitating the infrared pulse needed to turn off the TV. The sheet of paper he used in the video is this one here; just type this into the internet: " Universal standby something," and then you'll have to cut along these black lines. This quadrant here has 12 cuts, and these 12 cuts limit the infrared pulse needed to turn off the TV. The TV signal receiver is this little thing here. So you have to use the paper here in front of the lighter. It has to be lit because this is for turning off, not turning on, the lighter in front of the sensor. Look, there you see the reflection of the light on the sheet of paper. Now, pass it. Okay, quick, let's go... what could be happening? The science behind this might be theoretically correct, but it doesn't work for two reasons. The first is that this sheet of paper only has 12 cuts, and to mimic the correct frequency it would need 800 cuts and measure 18 meters, which is quite different, right? And the second reason it would n't work: to pulse the signal fast enough, I would have to pull this 18-meter sheet of paper at 400 km/h, and that's humanly impossible. But if you disregard these two small reasons, the theory behind the video is correct.
